coredata.dc:title: Double-passivated MAPbBr3 nanocrystals with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and polyvinylidene fluoride for white light LEDs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Yi
Tong, Feng
Chen, Zheng
Huang, Zijun
Huang, Sheng
Gu, Xiuquan
coredata.dc:description: This study addresses the challenge of synthesizing highly emissive and stable MAPbBr3 nanocrystals (NCs) for practical device applications. A double-passivation approach was developed through using nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) during the in-situ synthesis of MAPbBr3 NCs. By optimizing the amount of NGQDs, the resulting MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs composite films exhibited green emission at 522 nm with a narrow spectral width of 17.7 nm. The films demonstrated exceptional stability, retaining 60 % of their initial photoluminescence (PL) intensity after 144 h of water immersion and 96 % of their initial intensity after 10 min of heating at 100 °C. This stability is attributed to the NGQDs reducing defects and acting as nitrogen-enhanced sites that promote consistent crystal formation. Additionally, the composite film was used to fabricate a white light-emitting diode (WLED) with a color gamut coverage rate of 125 % under the National Television Standards Committee (NTSC) standard and 93.8 % under the Rec.2020 standard. This work might present a synergistic strategy combining in-situ defect passivation with encapsulation, offering a viable solution to the stability-efficiency trade-off in perovskite NCs and advancing high-color-gamut flexible displays.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite
MAPbBr3
NGQDs
Photoluminescence
WLED

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs. (a) XRD and (b) FTIR patterns. TEM images of (d) MAPbBr3 NCs and (e) NGQDs. SEM images of composite films (c) without and (e) with NGQDs.
XPS characterization. (a) total XPS survey. HRXPS profiles of (b) C1s, (c) N 1s, (d) Br 3d, (e) Pb 4f, (f) F 1s.
MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs films with different amounts of NGQDs. (a) PL, (b) FWHM, (c) TRPL and (d) PLQY plots.
PL spectra of (a) MAPbBr3/PVDF and (b) MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs composite films after immersion in water for various times. (c) Normalized PL intensity and (d) FWHM values as a function of days.

PL spectra of (a) MAPbBr3/PVDF films and (b) MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs films after heated at 100 °C for different times. (c) Normalized PL spectral intensity of the corresponding nanocomposite films. (d) FWHM curve.

(a) White light emission spectrum and the inset photograph shows the WLED under working condition. (b) Color gamut of as-fabricated WLED. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of preparing a MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs film

head.ce:title: Double-passivated MAPbBr3 nanocrystals with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and polyvinylidene fluoride for white light LEDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Physics, China University of Mining and Technology, Xuzhou, 221116, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Physics
China University of Mining and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Physics, China University of Mining and Technology, Xuzhou, 221116, People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Engineering and Technology, China University of Mining and Technology, Xuzhou, 221116, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China University of Mining and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical Engineering and Technology, China University of Mining and Technology, Xuzhou, 221116, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study addresses the challenge of synthesizing highly emissive and stable MAPbBr3 nanocrystals (NCs) for practical device applications. A double-passivation approach was developed through using nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) during the in-situ synthesis of MAPbBr3 NCs. By optimizing the amount of NGQDs, the resulting MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs composite films exhibited green emission at 522 nm with a narrow spectral width of 17.7 nm. The films demonstrated exceptional stability, retaining 60 % of their initial photoluminescence (PL) intensity after 144 h of water immersion and 96 % of their initial intensity after 10 min of heating at 100 °C. This stability is attributed to the NGQDs reducing defects and acting as nitrogen-enhanced sites that promote consistent crystal formation. Additionally, the composite film was used to fabricate a white light-emitting diode (WLED) with a color gamut coverage rate of 125 % under the National Television Standards Committee (NTSC) standard and 93.8 % under the Rec.2020 standard. This work might present a synergistic strategy combining in-situ defect passivation with encapsulation, offering a viable solution to the stability-efficiency trade-off in perovskite NCs and advancing high-color-gamut flexible displays.

Schematic illustration of preparing a MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs film.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, organic halide perovskites (OHPs) have gained significant attention for their exceptional optoelectronic properties, narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM), and cost-effective synthesis methods, making them highly attractive for applications in solar cells, light-emitting diodes, and optical sensors [1-4]. Among these materials, organic-inorganic halide perovskite MAPbBr3 NCs stand out due to their high PL efficiency, near-100 % quantum yields, tunable emission colors, and narrowband spectral output, which positions them as a promising candidate for advanced optical devices and LEDs [3-6]. However, the practical application of MAPbBr3 NCs in the luminescence fields remains hindered by two major challenges: low PL efficiency and poor environmental stability. These limitations stem primarily from the low formation energy of perovskites, which contributes to its instability, and surface-defect-induced quenching, which significantly reduces PL efficiency [7-10]. Addressing these issues is crucial for unlocking the full potential of MAPbBr3 NCs in next-generation optoelectronic devices.

To address the challenges of low PL efficiency and poor environmental stability in perovskite NCs, three main strategies have been developed: compositional engineering, matrix encapsulation, and surface passivation [11,12]. Among these, matrix encapsulation stands out as an effective and straightforward approach. Consequently, numerous encapsulation methods have been explored. The polymer layer effectively isolates the material from moisture, oxygen, and other external contaminants, thereby enhancing its environmental stability. Under thermal stress, this barrier helps to mitigate the direct impact of heat on the NCs, reducing lattice distortion or structural collapse caused by thermal vibrations. For instance, Wang et al. [13] utilized stepwise melting-polymerizing molecule (SMPM) as an additive to create a hydrophobic grain-level encapsulation layer for perovskite FAPbI3. This method facilitated multistage phase transitions during annealing, effectively mitigating moisture-induced degradation and lead leakage, and achieving a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 25.21 % in perovskite solar cells (PSCs). Similarly, Zhou et al. [14] encapsulated MAPbBr3 with PVDF, because the existed interactions between MA+ and -CF2- contributed to the crystallization control of MAPbBr3 NCs in PVDF matrix, enhancing moisture resistance and PL performance while maintaining high optical transparency in the as-fabricated films. Furthermore, the color gamut coverage of the fabricated WLED could reach 121 % of the NTSC standard. However, the long-term stability of the NCs under operational conditions and the color gamut uniformity remained suboptimal for commercial applications. Ngo et al. [15] synthesized polystyrene-encapsulated CsPbBr3 NCs through in situ polymerization of styrene monomers on polyacrylic acid-terminated NCs in polar solvents, resulting in materials with exceptional PL intensity, hydrothermal stability, fluorescence activity, and color purity. Despite these advancements, polymer-based encapsulation alone is insufficient to protect perovskite NCs from environmental stressors.

In order to overcome these limitations, surface passivation using ligands or inorganic oxides prior to encapsulation has been widely adopted [16]. For example, Ahmed et al. [17] applied YCl3 to passivate CsPbCl3 NCs, preserving their size and crystallinity while reducing surface trap states, leading to a 40 % improvement in the response time. Additionally, Jeong et al. [18] achieved simultaneous passivation of halogen vacancies and surface defects in CsPbBr3 NCs using Cd2+ and DDA+, substantially enhancing PL yield and external quantum efficiency. However, long-chain ligands often fail to bind effectively to perovskite NCs, leading to reaggregation and loss of quantum effects, while short-chain ligands provide a limited improvement of the stability. Therefore, it is crucial to develop stable, appropriately sized passivating materials for enhancing the stability of perovskite NCs. In this context, heteroatom-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs), particularly nitrogen-doped variants with ionic radii matching OHPs, have emerged as ideal candidates [19]. Recent studies highlight their potential in perovskite photovoltaics. For instance, F/N-co-doped GQDs achieved a PCE of 16.37 % in carbon-based cells through defect passivation and energy-level optimization [20], while Cl2-functionalized GQDs reduced surface defects for achieving a PCE of 18.91 %. However, the application of NGQDs in MAPbBr3 systems remains limited by their tendency to aggregate in polar solvents, which disrupts uniform dispersion during the NC synthesis process.

In this study, the in situ synthesis approach that combines NGQDs and PVDF was simultaneously used to passivate defects and encapsulate MAPbBr3 NCs. This single-step approach eliminates the need for conventional multi-step synthesis-passivation protocols, effectively preventing the aggregation of NGQDs and enhancing the environmental stability of MAPbBr3 NCs. The optimal loading amount of NGQDs was determined to be 12 μL, resulting in films with a narrow FWHM of 17.7 nm. Remarkably, after 144 h of water immersion, the films retained 60 % of their initial PL intensity, demonstrating exceptional stability. Additionally, the modified MAPbBr3 NCs embodied films also exhibited 60 % optical transparency and maintained structural integrity after 1 h of heating at 100 °C. To further validate the practical application of these films, a WLED was fabricated by integrating the NGQDs-modified MAPbBr3/PVDF film with a red K2SiF6: Mn4+ (KSF) phosphor film and a blue GaN LED. The optimized WLED device demonstrates a color gamut coverage of 125 % NTSC standard and 93.8 % Rec.2020 specification under CIE 1931 coordinates, representing a significant enhancement compared to commercial phosphor-converted WLEDs and exceeding the performance metrics reported for most perovskite-based LEDs in recent literature. Besides, the WLED exhibited chromaticity coordinates of (0.395, 0.461), while the green emission component achieved coordinates of (0.139, 0.783), closely matching the standard pure green coordinates (0.17, 0.797). These results underscore the film's capability for high-purity green light generation and its potential for advanced optoelectronic applications.

Ammonium citrate tribasic (C6H17N3O7, 98.5 %, Aladdin), Lead(II) bromide (PbBr2, 99 %, Macklin), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.9 %, Aladdin), Methylammonium bromide (MABr, 99.0 %, Polymer Light Technology Corp), Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, 99 %, average Mw ≈ 40,000, Macklin), K2SiF6:Mn4+ red phosphor (KSF, ZKHYC680A), Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS, (C2H6OSi)n, SYLGARD).

1g ammonium citrate was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water and heated to 200 °C using a heating mantle. After 20 min of reaction, the solution turned pale yellow, indicating the formation of NGQDs precursors. Next, 10 mL of an aqueous sodium hydroxide solution (10 mg/mL) was added to adjust the pH to approximately 7, yielding an aqueous NGQDs dispersion. Aliquots (0–60 μL) of this dispersion were then added to 10 mL of DMF. The mixture was heated to 100 °C for 3 min to evaporate any residual water and subsequently reconstituted to 10 mL with fresh DMF to obtain homogeneous NGQDs@DMF solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and modification of MAPbBr3 NCs
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1752g MAPbBr3 and 0.4404g PbBr2 were precisely weighed using an electronic balance. The powders were then dissolved in 15 mL of DMF and stirred at 70 °C and 700 rpm for 10 min, yielding a homogeneous MAPbBr3 precursor solution.

A sample bottle was placed on a magnetic stirrer, and 1 mL of MAPbBr3 precursor solution, 3 mL of NGQDs@DMF solution, 0.84 g of PVDF, and 2 mL of DMF were sequentially added. The mixture was stirred at 70 °C (700 rpm) for 10 min to obtain a homogeneous MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs solution. Subsequently, 1 mL of this solution was applied to a glass slide and uniformly spread into a 1000-μm-thick layer using a scraping technique; note that the film thickness can be adjusted by selecting an appropriate doctor blade. After drying completely, a smooth, transparent green film was obtained, which emitted a bright green fluorescence under UV light (380 nm). Scheme 1 illustrates the overall fabrication process, with an inset depicting the structure of the as-passivated MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs film.

Structural and compositional analyses were conducted using a suite of advanced characterization techniques. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Haoyuan DX-2700) with a Cu Kɑ source was employed to investigate crystallinity and phase purity, while field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi New Generation SU8220, Japan) provided detailed morphological insights. Chemical bonding and functional groups were analyzed using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker INVENIOR, 80 V), and elemental composition was determined via energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), which was assembled on the FESEM instrument. Surface chemical states were examined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi), while high-resolution imaging was performed with transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20). For optical characterization, ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis, Agilent Cary 300) was used to assess absorption properties, while PL spectroscopy (Cary 3000) was employed to evaluate the steady-state PL emission characteristics. Additionally, time-resolved PL (TRPL, Edinburgh Instruments FLS980) measurements were carried out to analyze both the fluorescence behavior and carrier lifetime in the MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs composites. The above comprehensive analyses provided critical insights into the structural, compositional, and optical properties of the samples.

In order to investigate the sample composition, the XRD measurement was conducted. Fig. 1 a shows the XRD patterns of pure PVDF, MAPbBr3/PVDF composite films, and MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs films with varying NGQDs content. For clarity, the standard XRD pattern of cubic MAPbBr3 crystals is included for structural comparison. The composite films exhibit distinct diffraction peaks at 15.16°, 21.36°, and 30.61°, which align with the (100), (011), and (200) crystal planes of cubic MAPbBr3 respectively [21,22], confirming that MAPbBr3 crystals have been successfully synthesized within the films. Notably, the peak intensities at 15.16° and 30.61° initially increase with rising NGQDs content, reaching a maximum at 12 μL before decreasing. These data suggest that moderate NGQDs incorporation enhances the crystallinity of MAPbBr3, possibly by stabilizing crystal nucleation through surface functional groups and promoting denser structures, whereas excess NGQDs likely induce aggregation, disrupt lattice continuity, and promote amorphous phase formation and phase separation [23,24]. Fig. 1b presents the FTIR spectra of pure PVDF, MAPbBr3/PVDF, and MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs films. A weak peak at 601 cm-1, attributed to Pb-Br lattice vibrations, is observed in MAPbBr3-containing films. In addition, a peak at 1671 cm-1, corresponding to N–H bending vibrations in ammonium groups (–NH3 +), is clearly discernible in the MAPbBr3/PVDF films, providing further evidence for the synthesis of MAPbBr3 [25]. Furthermore, the PVDF-related peaks at 511 and 832 cm-1 [14] are enhanced with increasing the addition amount of NGQDs, implying that NGQDs facilitate enhancing the crystallinity of the MAPbBr3/PVDF films. In order to characterize the morphology of MAPbBr3 NCs and NGQDs, the TEM characterization was performed. Figs. 1d, 1e and S1 provide HRTEM images of the MAPbBr3/NGQDs NCs. As shown in Fig. 1d, the MAPbBr3 NCs exhibit an average particle size of approximately 5 nm, which are uniformly dispersed within the PVDF matrix and the measured lattice spacing of 0.303 nm, which corresponds to the (200) interplanar distance of MAPbBr3 crystals [13]. Fig. 1e reveals lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.272 nm, which is consistent with the (100) plane spacing of NGQDs [26], thereby verifying their effective incorporation into the composite film. Fig. S1 further demonstrates the uniform distribution of MAPbBr3/PVDF, while the EDS mapping results in Fig. S2 indicate that the elements F, C, Br, Pb and N are enriched in the MAPbBr3 region. The SEM analysis was subsequently conducted to examine the structural impact of NGQDs further. Moreover, Figs. 1c and f compare the surface morphology of MAPbBr3/PVDF films with and without NGQDs. The result proves that the NGQDs-containing film shows significantly reduced surface porosity and improved structural uniformity, demonstrating the successful integration between NGQDs and MAPbBr3 crystals. In addition, Fig. S3a–f display the distribution of individual F, C, Br, Pb, and N elements in the MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs solution, further confirming that the MAPbBr3 NCs are successfully coated by NGQDs.

To further investigate the role of NGQDs, XPS was employed to analyze the surface chemical states. Fig. 2 a displays the survey spectra of N 1s, Pb 4f, C 1s, and F 1s for both samples, with calibration performed using the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) [27]. As shown in Fig. 2c, a 0.1 eV decrease in the N 1s binding energy upon NGQDs incorporation suggests enhanced structural stability due to a reduction in nitrogen bonding energy. Additionally, the broadening of the N 1s peak suggests potential interactions between NGQDs and -NH3+ groups in MAPbBr3 crystals [28,29]. In Fig. 2d, the Br 3d5/3 peak shifts from 66.89 eV to 66.99 eV after NGQDs passivation. This increase in binding energy suggests enhanced Br stability, which may help suppress the formation of Br vacancies. Concurrently, the Br 3d2/3 peak associated with Pb-Br bonds exhibits a 0.1 eV redshift, indicating stronger ionic bonding between Br− and Pb2+ ions following passivation [22]. Besides, as shown in Fig. 2e, Pb-related peaks undergo a 0.04 eV redshift after NGQDs incorporation, likely due to interactions between NGQDs and undercoordinated Pb atoms at the surface, effectively passivating these defect sites. Additionally, in Fig. 2f, the F 1s peak revealed a 0.09 eV redshift following NGQDs treatment, which is attributed to modified interactions between CH2 and CF2 groups that further enhance structural stability [14]. Collectively, these XPS results confirm the dual functionality of NGQDs: successful integration with MAPbBr3 crystals and effective passivation of surface defects.

To determine the optimal NGQDs concentration for green light emission in MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs films, steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy was used to investigate their PL properties. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the PL intensity increased with rising NGQDs concentration, reaching a maximum at 12 μL that delivered a twofold enhancement relative to the undoped sample. This result identifies 12 μL as the optimal NGQDs loading. Furthermore, NGQDs incorporation induced a redshift in the PL emission peak from 515 nm to 518–522 nm, shifting the emission toward the pure green spectrum (520–560 nm). The observed PL enhancement and spectral shift at low NGQDs concentrations can be attributed to surface defect passivation. Specifically, NGQDs effectively neutralize undercoordinated ions, reducing non-radiative recombination and improving both PL intensity and PLQY value [30-32]. However, when NGQDs loading exceeded 12 μL, a sharp decline in PL intensity was observed, likely due to lattice distortion within MAPbBr3 crystals. Overcrowding of NGQDs disrupts the perovskite’s crystallinity by introducing new defects that overpower the benefits of passivation. Moreover, the FWHM of the PL spectra progressively decreases with NGQDs incorporation, reaching a minimum of 17.76 nm at 12 μL, suggesting a more spectrally concentrated emission with enhanced monochromaticity [33]. This behavior is in agreement with the crystallinity improvements observed in XRD (Fig. 1a) as well as with the maximized PL intensity (Fig. 3a), collectively corroborating that 12 μL is the optimal NGQDs concentration for subsequent studies. Then, in order to further investigate the impact of NGQDs addition on defect passivation, the TRPL of the films before and after the addition of NGQDs was compared, as depicted in Fig. 3c and Table S1. The data, which was fitted with ExpDec2, revealed two distinct lifetime components: τ1 associated with charge trapping at defect sites and τ2 linked to radiative carrier recombination [34]. After passivation, the τ1 contribution decreased from 31.53 % to 16.29 %, indicating suppressed trap-assisted recombination, while the contribution of τ2 increased from 68.46 % to 83.71 %, reflecting improved bulk crystal quality and enhanced radiative efficiency [35]. In addition, the average carrier lifetime extended from 38.68 ns to 49.67 ns, providing direct evidence of reduced non-radiative losses. The enhancement in carrier lifetime may be attributed to the nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of the NGQDs, which passivate surface defects through chemical bonding.

Furthermore, UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. S4) revealed that the incorporation of NGQDs did not induce a significant change in the bandgap of the film, possibly owing to the small size of NGQDs. Moreover, because of the small size of the NCs, the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) occurred between layers. The transfer efficiency is determined by the change in the PL lifetime:
η
F R E T
= 1 −
τ
D A
/
τ D
Where τD is the lifetime of the pure donor (here is MAPbBr3) and τDA is the PL lifetime of the donor in the donor−acceptor mixture (here is MAPbBr3/NGQDs). The calculated FRET efficiency reached 22.13 %, confirming efficient energy transfer mediated by NGQDs [36]. Besides, PLQY measurements further support these findings (Figs. 3d, S4, and S5), with the NGQDs-passivated film achieving an increased PLQY from 35.9 % to 39.2 %. This enhancement may stem from two synergistic effects: reduced carrier trapping at passivated surface defects and improved photon recycling efficiency through prolonged carrier lifetimes [37,38]. Collectively, the TRPL and PLQY data establish that NGQDs save as multifunctional additives that simultaneously suppress defect states and optimize light emission dynamics.

To assess the stability of the prepared MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs, an experiment was conducted to evaluate their water stability under typical conditions (25 °C, 25 % RH). Figs. 4 a and b compare the PL stability of MAPbBr3/PVDF and MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs films during water immersion. The NGQDs-modified films demonstrated significantly slower PL intensity degradation compared to the control sample. Specifically, the unmodified MAPbBr3/PVDF film exhibited a 71.6 % PL intensity reduction after only 3 days of immersion, while the NGQDs-incorporated film retained 60 % of its initial PL intensity even after 7 days of exposure. Fig. 4c provides the normalized intensity of the PL curves, offering a more direct comparison of the data. These results confirm that NGQDs mitigate water-induced structural degradation of MAPbBr3, thereby enhancing its long-term stability. Fig. 4d tracks the normalized FWHM. The NGQDs-modified film maintained stable FWHM values for 6 days before any notable increase, whereas the control film showed rapid FWHM expansion starting on day 2. This FWHM trend aligns with the PL stability data, providing complementary evidence of NGQDs-enhanced water resistance. Moreover, water contact angle measurements (Fig. S7) revealed a progressive hydrophobicity enhancement with NGQDs incorporation. The optimal 12 μL NGQDs loading achieved a contact angle of 99°, indicating nearly superhydrophobic surface properties. This enhanced hydrophobicity forms a protective barrier against water penetration, significantly delaying moisture-induced degradation. These findings collectively demonstrate that NGQDs enhance water stability through dual mechanisms: hydrophobic surface modification to limit water contact and structural reinforcement to suppress MAPbBr3 crystal hydrolysis.

Thermal stability is also a critical performance metric for both display and LED application, as elevated temperatures accelerate oxygen- and moisture-induced material degradation. To assess thermal stabilization effect of NGQDs, we measured PL performance under heating at 100 °C for varying durations (Figs. 5 a-c). The unmodified film lost 89 % PL intensity after 60 min, whereas the NGQDs-passivated film showed only 73 % total loss at 60 min, indicating markedly slower degradation. These results confirm the effectiveness of NGQDs in suppressing the thermal decomposition of MAPbBr3 NCs. Consistent FWHM trends in Fig. 5d further corroborate the improved thermal stability. Furthermore, we quantified optical transparency changes post 1 h thermal aging at 100 °C, as nanocrystal decomposition critically impacts light transmission. As shown in Fig. S8 and S9, the NGQDs-passivated composite achieved 60 % transparency due to reduced light scattering. This enhancement correlates with NGQDs-induced surface densification, which suppresses light-scattering defects [39]. The passivated film retained 92 % of its initial transparency post-heating, outperforming the 90 % retention observed in the unmodified film. These findings collectively demonstrate that NGQDs substantially improve thermal resistance by mitigating the high-temperature decomposition of MAPbBr3.

Otherwise, we also placed the nanocomposite films in H2SO4 and NaOH for six days to investigate the material stability in acidic and alkaline environments (Fig. S10 and S11). The stability assessment revealed that MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs composite films maintained structural integrity in both alkaline and acidic environments. Specifically, the films showed 14 % attenuation of initial PL intensity after 48 h of alkaline immersion, which was significantly lower than the 33 % degradation observed in control samples under identical alkaline conditions. Notably, the films exhibited enhanced acid stability with 93 % PL intensity retention after 48-h acidic exposure. These comparative results establish the composite's superior chemical stability across extreme pH conditions compared to conventional perovskite formulations. Additionally, this research systematically investigates the influence of film thickness on PL properties, as shown in Fig. S12. The results demonstrate that when the film thickness varies within the range of 250 to 1000 nm, there is no significant impact on its luminescent characteristics. This insensitivity may be attributed to the defect-passivating role of NGQDs, which dominates over thickness-related light scattering effects.

Flexible displays have become a prominent research area in the display field [40,41], and among perovskite film applications in optical devices, backlight-based color conversion systems demonstrate significant potential. These systems achieve a wide color gamut by converting blue light into various wavelengths through thin-film spectral conversion [42,43]. Our design integrates a commercial GaN blue LED with two conversion layers: a MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs film for green light conversion and a KSF phosphor-PDMS composite film for red light emission, enabling white light through spectral blending. Fig. 6 a displays the synthesized white light spectrum, revealing three distinct emission peaks from the GaN blue LED (452 nm), perovskite- based green converter (522 nm), and KSF red phosphor (631 nm). The spectral separation between peaks exceeds 35 nm, ensuring minimal overlap. Moreover, the inset in Fig. 6a illustrates the prototype in operation, and Fig. 6b provides a quantitative analysis of color performance, with primary coordinates at (0.6934, 0.3064) for red, (0.1387, 0.7831) for green, and (0.1141, 0.0877) for blue. The white light coordinates (0.3951, 0.4611) approach ideal white emissions. Besides, the green component achieves exceptional purity at (0.1387, 0.7831), surpassing commercial phosphor-based systems by 18 % in color saturation. The color gamut coverage reaches 125 % of the NTSC standard and 93 % of Rec.2020 specifications, demonstrating unprecedented performance for next-generation displays.

Furthermore, in order to more intuitively demonstrate the advantages of this device compared to other WLEDs, we compared the parameters of WLEDs fabricated with various thin films, as shown in Table S2. Compared to commercial display light sources, the LEDs based on this composite system exhibit several notable advantages. For example, the synthesized light source exhibits reduced harmful blue light in the range from 400 nm to 450 nm. Additionally, using MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs as the luminescence layer allows for a narrow FWHM of only 17.7 nm, demonstrating excellent monochromatic independence. Besides, the MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs film shows remarkable stability against water, heat, and organic solvents. These breakthroughs establish MAPbBr3/PVDF/NGQDs film as a revolutionary material platform for advanced backlight units, particularly in ultra-high-definition flexible displays that require wide color reproduction.

In summary, MAPbBr3 NCs were passivated with NGQDs and encapsulated by PVDF, resulting in significantly enhanced optical properties and stability. The composite films exhibited a narrow green emission band centered at 522 nm with a FWHM of 17.7 nm and a PLQY of 39.2 %. Remarkably, the films demonstrated exceptional stability, retaining 60 % of their initial PL intensity after 144 h of water immersion and preserving 96 % of their PL intensity after heating at 100 °C for 10 min. This stability is attributed to the nitrogen-containing functional groups on NGQDs, which chemically integrate with undercoordinated Pb2+ sites on the perovskite surface, effectively passivating trap states and reducing non-radiative recombination. Furthermore, prototype WLEDs incorporating these films achieved CIE 1931 coordinates of (0.139, 0.783) for green emission, with a color gamut coverage reaching 125 % of the NTSC standard and 93.8 % of the Rec.2020 specification. These results underscore the potential of the developed luminescent composite films for high-color-purity lighting systems and advanced optoelectronic applications.

Yi Zhu: Writing – original draft. Feng Tong: Methodology, Investigation. Zheng Chen: Validation, Software. Zijun Huang: Formal analysis. Sheng Huang: Data curation. Xiuquan Gu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (52303356), Natural Science Fund project in Jiangsu Province (BK20210494).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mssp.2025.109700.
